narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your journey toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your bar exam odds—about a 47% chance of not passing—as too risky for admission right now. Picture it like this: the model looked at your story as a whole, and the biggest chapter that raised red flags was your Year 3 decile of 4, placing you in the lower middle of your class during that critical stretch, which pulled hardest toward predicting failure. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 5, showing a similar mid-pack performance early on that echoed concerns about building momentum. Your LSAT score of 34, while solid for many paths, didn't quite stand out enough here to counterbalance those trends, nudging the outlook downward. Coming from a middle-income family added another layer, as the model associates that bracket with tougher odds in this high-stakes arena. Your white background and undergraduate GPA of 3.3 each played supporting roles in tipping toward failure, painting a picture of steady but not accelerating academic drive. Even choosing part-time status factored in as a hurdle, suggesting divided focus might stretch your prep thin. The one bright spot was your gender as male, which gently pulled in the direction of passing, like a tailwind on a tough climb. Together, these pieces formed a narrative of potential that's real but needs more polish to confidently predict bar success. This isn't the end of your story—many with profiles like yours have retooled by boosting later grades or test scores. Consider targeting a stronger finish in upper years or retaking the LSAT to shift that prediction. You've got the foundation; a few targeted steps could rewrite this chapter entirely. Hang in there—we're rooting for your comeback.
